reviews.review_msg: David Firman was my t.a for a first year Cmns course. I will say I hated that man. I think he was too tough for 1st years. But now that I think about it, I think his harsh criticism really taught me something. I dropped communications because of him and now I'm taking it again.

I have mixed feeling for him, overall he display care for his students..os so it seems..but his way of teaching & explaining thing are so confusing. He runs through thing so fast. You are left with bigger confusion after class. Then again, math is not my forte.

He was my TA for one of my first post-secondary classes which was not helpful at all.  He seems like a nice guy at first, but when you go and ask him to clarify something, he goes on a completely different tangent only becoming really degrading. NOT recommended.

I thought he was a nice guy.I really did.Whenever I shared long convos w/him after lecture,he would appear to be so nice&caring.Turns out he's the opposite.Im hurt.Says below"when he thinks he's right he's right & he will explain that in terms of how wrong you are"-->very true.he's obnoxious/rude/not understanding/cannot teach/makes easy stuff hard
Coming right out of highschool this TA, in my opinion, wasn't very good. His complicated answers did little to help in this course.

The assignments he made always had so many mistakes on them.. like he couldn't be bothered to proofread his own assignments. 
 Questions are weird and ambiguous and don't ever trying to show how ambiguous they are because when he thinks he's right he's right.. adn he will explain that in terms of how wrong you are
David was "OKAY" but over all, this course was so unhelpful and hard.if u r not a "math" guy,don't ever!EVER! "EVER" take this course

Works really hard to help his students. He will answer any questions you have and is always available for help. Make sure you speak up and participate in his tutorials. Great effort, he should he a prof instead of a TA because he works hard and is really knowledgeable about the subject.
